facts.statement: Every day the Norse gods ride across Bifrost to hold council at the Well of Urd beneath Yggdrasil, where the three Norns — Urd, Verdandi, and Skuld — draw sacred water to pour over the world-tree and weave the fate of all beings.
facts.wowFactor: The water of the Well of Urd is so holy that anything submerged in it becomes as white as the inner lining of an eggshell. The Norns scoop it out daily and pour it over Yggdrasil mixed with the white clay around the well's edge, which is why Yggdrasil's bark is said to be pure white on the side the Norns tend. The Norse cosmos has a gardening schedule.
facts.visualDescription: A serene well beneath colossal roots, gods in gleaming armor arriving on horseback from a distant rainbow arc, three robed women moving quietly nearby drawing water in bowls, the whole scene lit by the cold light filtering down through miles of branch above

variants.question: The Norse gods hold their daily council at which sacred well beneath Yggdrasil?
The three Norns who determine the fate of all beings dwell beside which well?
True or false: The gods hold their daily council at the Well of Urd beneath Yggdrasil.
The sacred well where the Norns dwell and the gods hold daily council is the ___.

facts.quizQuestion: Which burning rainbow bridge will shatter when the sons of Muspell ride to Ragnarok?
facts.explanation: Bifrost's destruction is one of Ragnarok's most symbolic moments. The bridge has been the connection between gods and humanity throughout the entire age of the world, crossed daily by the gods riding to council, by Odin's ravens, by messengers and warriors. When Surtr's army crosses it, it cannot hold — and with its shattering, the permanent link between the divine and human worlds is severed in the same moment the gods themselves begin to fall. Heimdall, who has guarded the bridge through all of history, will have already blown the Gjallarhorn when he sees them coming.
facts.statement: Bifrost — the burning rainbow bridge that has connected Midgard to Asgard since the gods built it — is prophesied to shatter when the sons of Muspelheim ride across it at Ragnarok, the bridge unable to bear the weight of the world's end.
facts.wowFactor: Some scholars believe Bifrost was not originally conceived as a rainbow but as the Aurora Borealis — 'Bifrost' possibly means 'the flickering bridge,' and the Northern Lights would have appeared to a Viking as a shimmering three-colored arc spanning from the dark earth to the heavens. If so, every Northern Lights display was a reminder that the bridge between worlds was still standing — and still burning.
facts.visualDescription: A shimmering three-colored arc of fire seen from below as it trembles — massive armored figures beginning to cross from one end, the bridge bowing, cracks of darkness appearing in the burning light, a lone watchman raising a horn to his lips at the other end

variants.question: The bridge between Asgard and Midgard, fated to shatter at Ragnarok, is called what?
Which god guards the bridge Bifrost against the giants?
True or false: Bifrost is prophesied to shatter when the sons of Muspell ride across it at Ragnarok.
The burning rainbow bridge between Asgard and Midgard, guarded by Heimdall, is ___.

facts.quizQuestion: Who was licked free from ice by the cow Audhumla, becoming grandfather of Odin?
facts.explanation: Buri's emergence from the ice is one of the most patient acts in world mythology: three days of a cow's tongue working at salty stone to reveal a divine being one body-part at a time. Buri fathered Borr, who with the giantess Bestla — daughter of the giant Bolthorn — produced the three brothers who built the world. This means Odin is part giant on his mother's side, a fact that complicates the simple gods-versus-giants narrative. Buri himself appears only in this single scene; once he has fathered Borr and set the divine lineage in motion, the sources never mention him again.
facts.statement: Buri is the first god — licked free from rime ice by the primordial cow Audhumla over three days, his hair appearing first, then his head, then his full form — and is the grandfather of Odin, Vili, and Ve.
facts.wowFactor: Buri's name means 'Producer' or 'Father' in Old Norse — he is so fundamentally the origin point that he has no personality, no deeds, and no further story. He exists only to be the first. In a mythology full of gods with elaborate personalities and histories, the grandfather of Odin is a cipher: a man who stepped out of the ice and produced the builders of the world, then vanished from the record.
facts.visualDescription: A powerful figure of calm authority emerging from a block of ancient frost-blue ice, each pass of the cow's tongue revealing more of him, the void around him so primordially empty that the emergence of a single conscious being is the most dramatic event that has yet occurred

variants.question: Buri, the grandfather of Odin, was revealed when which primordial cow licked the ice?
How many days did it take Audhumla to lick Buri completely free from the rime stones?
True or false: Buri, the first god, was licked free from rime ice by the cow Audhumla over three days.
The first god, grandfather of Odin, who was licked free from rime ice, is ___.

facts.quizQuestion: Which god, along with brothers Vili and Ve, shaped the world from Ymir's body?
facts.explanation: The three brothers are not equals in the Norse sources — Odin dominates — but their three-fold structure is significant. Odin (spirit/inspiration), Vili (will/consciousness), and Ve (holiness/sanctuary) together constitute a complete act of creation: raw creative energy, directed intention, and sacred grounding. They gave Ask and Embla different gifts matching their names: Odin breath and life, Vili intelligence and movement, Ve warmth and speech. The brothers also appear in another key myth from the Trickery, Fate & Runes sub-deck: while Odin was away, Vili and Ve ruled Asgard and reportedly lived with Frigg — a detail the sources introduce without explanation and never resolve.
facts.statement: Odin, Vili, and Ve — sons of Borr and the giantess Bestla — killed Ymir and shaped the world from his body, their three names meaning Inspiration, Will, and Holiness: a divine triad of spirit, cognition, and sacred power.
facts.wowFactor: The three brothers' names function as a philosophical taxonomy of creative power: Odin (the inspired mind), Vili (the directed will), Ve (the consecrated space). When Snorri Sturluson wrote down the creation myth in the 13th century, he was preserving what may be a very old Indo-European tripartite structure — spirit, will, and sacred order — translated into three brothers with a giant's body and a world to build.
facts.visualDescription: Three godlike figures of similar height but different presence — one crackling with restless energy, one with focused intent, one radiating a quiet consecration — working in deliberate concert over a vast fallen body, each doing what their nature demands

variants.question: Odin shaped the world from Ymir's body alongside which two brothers?
What did Odin, Vili, and Ve use to create the dome of the sky?
True or false: Odin, Vili, and Ve created the world from the body of the slain giant Ymir.
The oldest of the three brothers who shaped the world from Ymir's body is ___.

facts.quizQuestion: Which realm was built from Ymir's eyebrows to protect humanity from the giants?
facts.explanation: The choice of eyebrows for Midgard's wall is one of the most specific and vivid details in Norse creation: of all the parts of Ymir's enormous body available to them, the three brothers used the eyebrows to define the boundary of human civilization. The symbolism works: eyebrows are the first line of defense for the eyes, framing vision and protecting perception. Midgard — the realm of the beings the gods created from trees — is protected by the outermost edge of the giant whose death made the world. The same fact appears from a different angle in the Realms sub-deck: Midgard means 'Middle Enclosure,' and this is the enclosure.
facts.statement: Midgard's protective wall was fashioned from Ymir's eyebrows by Odin, Vili, and Ve — a boundary between the new human realm and the hostile giants of the outer world, enclosing the space where Ask and Embla would live.
facts.wowFactor: Some sources say it was Ymir's eyelashes rather than eyebrows that formed Midgard's walls — a discrepancy that has bothered scholars for centuries. Norse mythology was oral for hundreds of years before being written down, and small details drifted between tellings. The exact body part is uncertain; what is certain is that the wall of the human world was made from the face of the giant who became everything else.
facts.visualDescription: A ring of dense grey-brown material forming a perfect circle around a green interior, outside it the grey-blue of a hostile frozen wilderness, inside it a sheltered world still being arranged into forests and rivers, the wall's origin in something living visible in its irregular texture

variants.question: Ymir's eyebrows were fashioned into a wall to create which realm for humans?
What body part of Ymir was used to create the protective wall of Midgard?
True or false: Midgard was built from Ymir's eyebrows to protect humans from the frost giants.
The protective enclosure built from Ymir's eyebrows to shelter humanity is ___.

facts.quizQuestion: Which two wolves chase the Sun and Moon in their chariots across the sky?
facts.explanation: The Norse cosmos personified celestial bodies as beings in motion, not fixed points. Sol (Sun) drives her chariot faster and faster across the sky because Skoll is always behind her; Mani (Moon) races through his phases partly because Hati drives him. Norse eclipses were understood as these wolves momentarily catching their prey before being frightened off. The wolves are sons of Fenrir and an unnamed giantess of the Ironwood, meaning they are grandchildren of Loki — the trickster's bloodline extends from the world serpent Jormungandr to the hell-queen Hel to the wolf Fenrir to these two wolves who will extinguish the lights of the sky.
facts.statement: The sun and moon are driven across the sky by the goddess Sol and the god Mani in chariots, pursued by the wolves Skoll and Hati — sons of Fenrir — who will finally swallow them at the opening of Ragnarok.
facts.wowFactor: Hati means 'He Who Hates' and Skoll means 'Treachery' — every sunrise and sunset, the Norse were watching the celestial embodiment of hatred and treachery pursuing the light across the sky. They built an entire cosmology around the idea that the light exists in permanent flight from the forces trying to extinguish it. Most days the light wins. Eventually it won't.
facts.visualDescription: Two enormous coal-black wolves in full sprint across a deep blue sky, one bounding after a blazing golden chariot trailed by light, the other closing on a silver disc pulled by a pale horse, the chariots pushing faster as the jaws snap closer

variants.question: Skoll chases the Sun and Hati chases the Moon — what are both Skoll and Hati?
What is the name of the giantess who gave birth to Skoll and Hati, dwelling in the Ironwood?
True or false: The wolves Skoll and Hati chase the Sun and Moon, fated to swallow them at Ragnarok.
The two wolves that chase the Sun and Moon across the sky are Skoll and ___.

facts.quizQuestion: Which beings were created from maggots in Ymir's flesh by Odin, Vili, and Ve?
facts.explanation: The dwarves' origin is the most grotesque in Norse mythology: the master forgers of Mjolnir, Gungnir, Gleipnir, and Draupnir — the beings who effectively equipped the entire Norse pantheon for everything including Ragnarok — began as insects in a corpse. The gods gave them intelligence and form, but they retained the nature of their origin: creatures of darkness and underground spaces, averse to sunlight (which turns them to stone), oriented entirely toward craft and material. Their realm Svartalfheim sits in the roots of the world-tree, directly above the darkest place in the cosmos. Their greatest creation, Gleipnir, was the only thing that could hold Fenrir — built from impossible ingredients by impossible beings from an impossible origin.
facts.statement: The dwarves were created when maggots formed in Ymir's rotting flesh after his death — Odin, Vili, and Ve gave them human understanding and appearance, and they descended into Svartalfheim to become the cosmos's greatest craftsmen.
facts.wowFactor: The beings who forged the most powerful weapons in Norse mythology began as maggots in a dead giant's flesh. There is no other tradition in world mythology where the divine armory was manufactured by the transformed larvae of decay. The Norse found cosmic craftsmanship not in heavenly fires but in the organic process of decomposition becoming creation — a cycle as fundamental as Ragnarok itself.
facts.visualDescription: Dark stone coming alive as small forms push through from inside, taking shape with hands already reaching for tools, the underground caverns they emerge into already holding the raw materials of a cosmos that needs equipping, forge-light appearing as if the rock itself is remembering how to burn

variants.question: Dwarves were created from maggots in the body of which primordial giant?
What did the gods give to the maggots in Ymir's flesh to transform them into dwarves?
True or false: Dwarves were created from maggots in Ymir's flesh after the gods gave them human understanding.
The beings created from maggots in Ymir's flesh, who became master craftsmen, are the ___.

facts.quizQuestion: Which wolf's jaws stretch from earth to sky when freed at Ragnarok?
facts.explanation: This image from the Prose Edda is one of the most spectacular in Norse mythology: a wolf so large that its open mouth frames the entire vertical extent of the cosmos. The gods spent an entire age preventing exactly this moment — two broken iron chains, one magical ribbon, Tyr's hand, an island called Lyngvi, untold years of delay — and at Ragnarok every measure fails simultaneously. Fenrir's size also explains why only Vidar, Odin's son who specializes in silence and endurance rather than combat, can deal with him: you cannot fight something that large with weapons. You have to get inside its mouth.
facts.statement: When Fenrir breaks his bonds at Ragnarok, his upper jaw touches the sky and his lower jaw scrapes the earth — jaws wide enough to swallow the heavens — and he advances on the gods with the Gleipnir ribbon trailing broken from his limbs.
facts.wowFactor: The Eddas say that if Fenrir had not been bound, he would have devoured everything before Ragnarok. The entire age of the Norse gods — all the myths of Thor fighting giants, Odin gathering wisdom, Freyja loving and mourning — exists inside the parentheses of Fenrir's binding and Fenrir's release. The whole world was an interval between one wolf being tied down and that same wolf standing up.
facts.visualDescription: An open maw that fills the entire frame — lower jaw of cracked stone and mud, upper jaw of storm clouds — the wolf advancing through a landscape of broken gods and burning things, a single silken ribbon hanging from the lower jaw in tatters

variants.question: Fenrir kills Odin at Ragnarok — which god then avenges Odin by killing Fenrir?
What is the name of the magical chain that bound Fenrir before Ragnarok?
True or false: At Ragnarok, Fenrir's freed jaws are described as stretching from earth to sky.
The wolf whose jaws stretch from earth to sky at Ragnarok is ___.

facts.quizQuestion: Which Ragnarok ship is built entirely from the untrimmed fingernails of the dead?
facts.explanation: Naglfar is described as the largest ship ever built, captained by the giant Hrym at Ragnarok and carrying Loki — now free from his mountain prison — and an army of the dead. The ship's construction is continuous: every corpse interred with untrimmed nails contributes material. This created a genuine religious practice: Norse funerary custom included nail-trimming as a specific obligation, not of hygiene but of cosmological duty. The myth makes each individual death a small political act — cooperate with the end or withhold your nails. It is one of the most practically motivated pieces of Norse cosmology, directly connecting the living's behavior to the speed of the apocalypse.
facts.statement: Naglfar is built entirely from the untrimmed fingernails and toenails of the dead — every clipped nail that was not cut before burial going into its hull — and the Prose Edda warns the living to trim their dead's nails to delay the apocalypse.
facts.wowFactor: The Gylfaginning passage reads: 'It is worth warning people against giving a person long nails in death, since it is from this material that the ship called Naglfar is made.' Norse mythology is one of the only religious traditions in the world where an apocalyptic prophecy comes with a specific and actionable nail-hygiene instruction.
facts.visualDescription: A close view of Naglfar's hull seen from the water level — pale overlapping fingernails and toenails of every size, from every age and body, fitted together with terrible patience into an unbroken surface, the ship moving silently through black water with the weight of everything that was not prepared for

variants.question: Naglfar, the doomsday ship, is made from which material?
Who captains Naglfar when it sails to Ragnarok?
True or false: Naglfar is built from the untrimmed nails of the dead, and sailing it to Ragnarok is its purpose.
The Ragnarok ship built from dead men's fingernails and toenails is ___.

facts.quizQuestion: During which cataclysm do the stars vanish from the sky in Norse myth?
facts.explanation: The Voluspa describes Ragnarok's unfolding as a sequence of lights going out: first the sun (swallowed by Skoll), then the moon (swallowed by Hati), then the stars fading from the heavens. By the time Surtr raises his flaming sword, there is no other light source remaining in the entire cosmos. The Norse cosmology that began with fire from Muspelheim and ice from Niflheim colliding in Ginnungagap ends with fire from Muspelheim consuming everything — the cosmos returning to its two primordial ingredients. Then the world sinks. Then it rises. The stars that vanish in this age reappear in the next.
facts.statement: During Ragnarok, the stars vanish from the sky as part of the cosmic unraveling — following the sun and moon's swallowing by Skoll and Hati, the darkness becomes total before Surtr's sword provides the last light.
facts.wowFactor: The Norse term Ragnarok literally means 'the fate of the gods' (from regin, 'the ruling powers,' and rok, 'fate, doom') — but an alternate interpretation is Ragnarokkr, 'the twilight of the gods,' from rokkr meaning 'darkness.' Both readings are correct simultaneously: the gods' fate is darkness. Wagner borrowed 'Twilight of the Gods' (Götterdämmerung) from this alternate reading, giving modern culture its most famous name for an apocalypse.
facts.visualDescription: A night sky seen from earth as stars blink out one by one from horizon to horizon, each disappearance visible as a small extinguishing, the sky darkening beyond even night, the only remaining light a line of orange fire at one edge of the world that is getting larger

variants.question: Stars vanishing from the sky is one sign of which Norse cataclysm?
What are the three consecutive winters with no summer between them called, which precede Ragnarok?
True or false: During Ragnarok, the stars vanish from the sky as part of the cosmic destruction.
The cataclysmic end of the current Norse world, during which stars vanish from the sky, is ___.

facts.quizQuestion: What do surviving gods find in the grass after the world is reborn following Ragnarok?
facts.explanation: The Voluspa ends with this image: the new world's gods — including Baldur returned from Helheim, and Vidar and Vali, and the sons of Thor — meeting on Idavoll (the same plain where Asgard once stood) and finding the golden game pieces the old gods had played with before everything ended. It is one of mythology's most quietly devastating images. The game pieces are not treasure or weapons — they are toys, leisure objects, evidence that the gods before Ragnarok had time to play. The survivors pick them up from the new grass of a world that does not yet know what happened to the last one.
facts.statement: After Ragnarok ends and the world rises clean from the sea, the surviving gods gather on the new fields and find golden playing pieces in the fresh grass — relics of the age before, found by beings returning to something they had to destroy to keep.
facts.wowFactor: The golden game pieces are the single most poignant object in Norse mythology. The gods of the old world sat around and played games — board games with golden pieces, on the green fields before anything had gone wrong. Everything that follows in the mythology, every sacrifice, every battle, every careful preparation for an end they cannot prevent, was the same beings who once played games in the sun. After everything, the survivors find the pieces and presumably begin again.
facts.visualDescription: A god kneeling in new green grass, picking up a small golden disc that still glints like it was made yesterday, the others behind them beginning to notice more of them scattered through the field, a clear sky above a world with no ruins visible, only the grass and the pieces and the question of what to do next

variants.question: Golden playing pieces found in the grass symbolize what event in Norse myth?
variants.correctAnswer: The rebirth of the world after Ragnarok

facts.quizQuestion: Which god snuck up on Sif while she slept and cut off all her golden hair?
facts.explanation: Loki's prank on Sif is the most consequential act of pure mischief in Norse mythology. Thor's murderous fury forced Loki to commission the Sons of Ivaldi in Svartalfheim, who produced: magical gold hair for Sif, Gungnir for Odin, and Skidbladnir for Freyr. Loki then rashly wagered his own head against brothers Brokkr and Sindri — producing Gullinbursti the golden boar, Draupnir the self-multiplying gold ring, and Mjölnir. All six artifacts are described in more detail in this deck's other facts. Sif's hair itself is possibly a wheat-field allegory — its golden replacement growing back like a field after blight — and Loki's midnight scissors may encode a mythological memory of failed harvests and their aftermath.
facts.statement: Loki cut off Sif's golden hair for no reason except that he could — and in scrambling to fix the damage, accidentally triggered the creation of Mjölnir, Gungnir, Gleipnir, Skidbladnir, and more.
facts.wowFactor: Loki's senseless prank produced six divine artifacts that collectively armed Odin, protected Freyr's kingship, built the chain that would eventually bind Fenrir, and gave Thor the weapon he uses to kill Jörmungandr. Pure mischief accidentally equipped the gods for the end of the world. Loki is simultaneously Norse mythology's greatest villain and its most productive catastrophe.
facts.visualDescription: A trickster with gleaming shears crouching over a sleeping golden-haired goddess in a dim Asgard hall, a pile of shorn hair glowing faintly beside him — and beneath the scene, visible through the floor as if through glass, an underground forge already lit with volcanic light where small bearded craftsmen begin to work
facts.sourceName: Wikipedia — Sif; Wikipedia — Gungnir (Skáldskaparmál, Prose Edda)

variants.question: Loki cut the golden hair of which goddess while she slept?
After cutting Sif's hair, which craftsmen did Loki commission to forge gold replacement hair?
True or false: Loki cut off Sif's golden hair as a prank, then arranged for dwarves to make replacement gold hair.
While Sif slept, ___ crept up and sheared off all her famous golden hair.

facts.quizQuestion: Which god bet his own head against dwarves Brokkr and Sindri, and lost?
facts.explanation: Having obtained three treasures from the Sons of Ivaldi (Sif's hair, Gungnir, Skidbladnir), Loki boasted to the brothers Brokkr and Sindri that they could not match this work. He wagered his head on it. Brokkr and Sindri began: Sindri worked while Brokkr pumped the bellows and was told not to stop for any reason. Loki transformed into a fly. He bit Brokkr's hand twice during the boar-forging, and the work continued. On the third item — the hammer — he bit Brokkr's eyelid so savagely that Brokkr flinched for just a moment, and Mjölnir's handle came out too short. The gods judged Mjölnir the most valuable gift of all six — the short handle was a flaw, not a disqualification — and Loki lost his head. He escaped beheading by arguing the wager covered his head but not his neck. The dwarves sewed his lips shut.
facts.statement: Loki bet his own head against dwarves Brokkr and Sindri — and lost, because the hammer they forged was judged the most valuable of all six divine artifacts, and Mjölnir's short handle was Loki's own sabotage mid-forging.
facts.wowFactor: Loki sabotaged Mjölnir mid-forging with an insect bite — and still lost the bet, because even a flawed Mjölnir was judged more valuable than everything else combined. The most feared weapon in the cosmos has a design defect because of a trickster's bite, and the trickster still lost despite causing it. Loki could not even sabotage himself into victory.
facts.visualDescription: Two stocky dwarves at a volcanic forge, Sindri hammering the unmistakable shape of a hammer while Brokkr pumps bellows — and hovering near Brokkr's ear, the tiny shape of a fly, positioned to strike — the forge light catching the developing weapon on the anvil, its handle already too short

variants.question: Which dwarves won the wager against Loki and sewed his lips shut?
Which of the six dwarf-forged gifts did the gods judge most valuable, sealing Loki's loss?
True or false: Loki wagered his head against dwarves and then had his lips sewn shut when he lost.
___ bet his own head that no dwarf could out-craft the Sons of Ivaldi, but lost the wager.

facts.quizQuestion: What eight-legged horse was born after Loki disguised himself as a mare to lure away the builder's stallion?
facts.explanation: The wall-builder's contract promised him Freyja, the sun, and the moon if he could finish Asgard's walls in a single winter using only his stallion Svadilfari. The gods — on Loki's advice — assumed he would fail. As the deadline approached, it became clear Svadilfari's supernatural work pace would meet it. With three days remaining, Loki transformed into a beautiful mare and led Svadilfari away from the work site, into the forest, for the rest of the season. The builder — revealed as a Jötunn in disguise — threatened the gods and was killed by Thor. Loki, still as a mare, disappeared into the wilderness and returned months later with a grey foal of extraordinary form: eight-legged Sleipnir, now Odin's mount and the best horse in all nine worlds. The Trickery sub-deck returns to Sleipnir in the Creatures sub-deck's myth_norse_sleipnir_parentage and myth_norse_sleipnir_best_horse facts.
facts.statement: Loki transformed into a mare to save the gods from an impossible wager — lured the builder's stallion into the forest, disrupted the wall construction, and months later gave birth to Sleipnir.
facts.wowFactor: The Prose Edda records the birth of Sleipnir with complete matter-of-factness, as if a shapeshifting male deity gestating and birthing a foal after seducing a stallion requires no editorial comment whatsoever. Snorri Sturluson, writing in 13th-century Iceland, seems to have found this no more remarkable than any other divine event. Norse mythology has a wider definition of the possible.
facts.visualDescription: A silver-grey mare galloping through moonlit forest with a massive stallion in pursuit, the mare trailing rune-light from its hooves, both disappearing into deep shadow — and months later, from a forest glade, a small grey foal with eight legs stepping into the dawn light for the first time

variants.question: Sleipnir was born after Loki disguised himself as which type of animal?
How many legs did Sleipnir, the greatest of all horses, have?
True or false: Loki is the biological mother of Sleipnir, Odin's horse.
Loki gave birth to ___, the eight-legged horse, after shape-shifting into a mare.

facts.quizQuestion: Which giant captured Loki mid-flight and forced him to lure Idunn out of Asgard?
facts.explanation: Loki, Odin, and Hœnir were traveling when a large eagle stole their food. Loki struck it with a pole that became magically bound to the eagle's body — the eagle was Thiazi, a storm giant in disguise. Dragged helplessly through the air, Loki swore to deliver Idunn and her apples into Thiazi's hands. He tricked Idunn into leaving Asgard's protection by telling her he had seen fruit finer than hers in the forest and that she should bring her apples to compare. Thiazi took her to his mountain hall Þrymheimr. Without her apples, the gods — who normally ate one each day to maintain their immortality — began aging rapidly. Grey hair, stooped backs, failing eyes. Odin, Thor, Freyr — all growing old for the first time. Loki was forced to retrieve her, transforming into a falcon to carry her back while Thiazi pursued.
facts.statement: The giant Thiazi captured Loki in eagle-form and extorted him into delivering Idunn — and without her apples, the gods began visibly aging toward death for the first time.
facts.wowFactor: After the gods killed Thiazi in pursuit, his daughter Skaði armored herself and marched on Asgard demanding compensation for her father's death. She won: she was offered a god for a husband, and was allowed to choose by examining only feet. She chose the feet she thought were Baldr's — they belonged to Njörðr. Their marriage failed because she wanted mountains and he wanted the sea, a story told in the Aesir sub-deck. Even Thiazi's defeat produced consequences that outlasted the myth.
facts.visualDescription: An enormous dark eagle in full stoop from a grey sky, talons extended toward a golden-haired goddess standing in a forest glade with a wooden chest, its wings blocking the sun — behind the goddess, visible through the trees, the walls of Asgard she has just left behind
facts.sourceName: Wikipedia — Þjazi (Skáldskaparmál, Prose Edda; Haustlöng, 9th century)

variants.question: Thiazi kidnapped which goddess who guarded the apples of immortality?
What disguise did the giant Thiazi use when he first captured Loki?
True or false: Loki willingly helped Thiazi kidnap Idunn after being physically captured and coerced by oath.
The giant ___ disguised himself as an eagle to capture Loki and extort him into kidnapping Idunn.

facts.quizQuestion: Which god crashed Aegir's feast and systematically insulted every god present?
facts.explanation: Loki had been excluded from Ægir's feast for killing the servant Fimafengr. He returned anyway, invoking his blood-brotherhood with Odin (the mixing of their blood in an ancient compact) to demand hospitality — a bond Odin could not refuse without violating his own law. Once seated, Loki worked around the table: accusing Óðinn of being a poor arbiter of battle-luck, Frigg of infidelity, Skaðhiöfr of cowardice, Iðunn of sleeping with her brother's killer, Gefjun of trading favors for a necklace, Freyja of sleeping with every god and elf in the hall, Njörðr of fathering children by his sister, Freyr of giving away his sword, Tyr of losing his hand and failing to reconcile anything. Thor arrived last and threatened Loki repeatedly until he finally left — but left voluntarily, having said everything he came to say.
facts.statement: Loki crashed Ægir's feast by invoking his blood-oath with Odin for a seat — and then methodically humiliated every god in attendance with accusations they couldn't deny.
facts.wowFactor: Odin could not expel Loki because they had sworn blood-brotherhood in an ancient oath — the legal and social obligation of that bond was absolute even between enemies. Loki weaponized Odin's own sense of divine law against him. The Allfather, who sacrificed everything for wisdom and order, was forced to grant a seat to his enemy and watch him destroy every reputation in the hall, unable to act, because his own principles bound his hands.
facts.visualDescription: A grinning red-haired figure standing at the center of a long mead hall with a raised horn, pointing at each god in turn — some with hands on weapons, some with downcast eyes, some red-faced with fury or humiliation — while at the far end of the table, a one-eyed king sits still, expression unreadable, unable to move
facts.sourceName: Wikipedia — Lokasenna (Codex Regius, c.1270; Poetic Edda)

variants.question: The poem Lokasenna is set at a feast held in whose hall?
Which god's arrival finally ended Loki's insult rampage at Aegir's feast?
True or false: In Lokasenna, Loki crashed Aegir's feast and insulted every god in turn before Thor drove him away.
The Eddic poem ___ depicts Loki crashing a divine feast and trading insults with twelve gods.

facts.quizQuestion: Which god hung on Yggdrasil for nine nights, wounded by a spear, to discover the runes?
facts.explanation: The Hávamál's stanzas 138 and 139 are among the most extraordinary passages in world mythology: 'I know that I hung on the windswept tree / nine full nights / wounded with a spear / consecrated to Óðinn / myself to myself / on that tree / which no one knows / from what roots it grows.' No one helped him. No one gave him food or water or comfort. He was simultaneously the god, the priest, the victim, and the beneficiary of the sacrifice. On the ninth night, he saw the runes emerging in the darkness below him and seized them with a cry. The runes then became available through Odin to any human disciplined enough to learn them, as covered in myth_norse_odin_runes and the broader context of the Trickery sub-deck.
facts.statement: Odin hung himself on Yggdrasil for nine nights — wounded, starving, sacrificed to himself by himself — and on the ninth night the runes revealed themselves in the dark below him.
facts.wowFactor: The phrase 'myself to myself' encodes a paradox that Norse theology seems comfortable with and which most other theologies would consider self-contradictory: Odin as supreme god cannot offer himself to a higher authority, so he offers himself to himself. The sacrifice is valid. The wisdom is obtained. The Norse did not require a recipient outside the self to make a sacrifice meaningful — suffering and transformation were their own justification.
facts.visualDescription: A lone figure hanging in pure darkness from a vast tree, a spear wound at his side, arms spread, face tilted down toward the void — and in the void below, rising slowly toward his outstretched hand, the glowing angular shapes of the first runes, emerging from nothing as he watches
facts.sourceName: Wikipedia — Gungnir (Hávamál, Poetic Edda); Wikipedia — Odin

variants.question: Odin hung on Yggdrasil for how many nights to discover the runes?
In which Eddic poem does Odin describe his self-sacrifice on Yggdrasil to obtain the runes?
True or false: Odin hung on Yggdrasil for nine nights, wounded by a spear, as a self-sacrifice to gain knowledge of the runes.
___ hung from Yggdrasil nine nights, wounded by a spear, offering himself to himself to learn the runes.

facts.quizQuestion: About 3,000 carved stones bearing runic inscriptions survive across Scandinavia — what are they called?
facts.explanation: Rune stones were erected primarily between the 4th and 12th centuries CE, with the greatest concentration in the Viking Age (800–1100 CE). Most were memorial stones — raised by families to honor dead relatives, sometimes recording journeys, military service, or inheritance claims. The oldest runic inscriptions date to the 2nd century CE. Sweden's Uppland province alone has more than 1,200 surviving rune stones. The runes on these stones connect directly to Odin's discovery of the runes on Yggdrasil — every inscription is implicitly a use of knowledge that cost the Allfather nine nights of suffering and his own spear-wound.
facts.statement: About 3,000 rune stones survive across Scandinavia — Sweden alone holds roughly 2,500 — making Scandinavian rune stones one of the largest surviving bodies of pre-Christian inscribed monuments on earth.
facts.wowFactor: Sweden has more surviving rune stones than all other countries combined. You can drive through Uppland today and see them in farmers' fields, embedded in church walls, propped up by roadsides — the actual, physical, carved-stone afterlife of a mythology. When the Norse said Odin gave humans the runes, they meant something that can still be touched with bare hands in a Swedish field in the twenty-first century.
facts.visualDescription: A tall weathered granite boulder standing in a field of frost-dusted grass at dawn, covered in interlacing serpent forms encircling sharp angular rune characters, a faint blush of old red pigment still visible in the deepest carved grooves — ancient, intact, readable, present
facts.sourceName: Wikipedia — Odin; Wikipedia — Gungnir (Sigrdrífumál rune references)

variants.question: Roughly how many rune stones survive across Scandinavia?
The oldest known runic inscriptions date from which century?
True or false: Approximately 3,000 rune stones survive across Scandinavia, the oldest from the 2nd century CE.
About 3,000 ___ survive across Scandinavia, bearing inscriptions in the runic alphabet.

facts.quizQuestion: Which male god was mocked for practicing seidr, a form of magic considered shameful for men?
facts.explanation: Seidr was taught to the Aesir by Freyja, who came to Asgard as part of the Aesir-Vanir war settlement. It involved an elevated platform, a staff, a ritual trance induced by chanting women, and the ability to perceive and potentially manipulate the threads of fate. It was called ergi to practice it as a man — a word meaning unmanliness or sexual passivity, deeply shameful in the Norse honor culture. Odin embraced it anyway. He learned it from Freyja. He practiced it on the island of Samsø in women's attire, according to Snorri. Loki accused him of this publicly in the Lokasenna, covered in this sub-deck. Odin's response was to accept the shame because seidr gave him something more valuable: proximity to the Norns' weaving, a chance to understand the fate-threads that even his rune-knowledge couldn't fully read.
facts.statement: Odin practiced seidr — fate-magic coded as a woman's discipline — and was mocked by Loki for it as unmanly, because Odin valued power over honor, always.
facts.wowFactor: Odin consistently sacrifices social standing for power. He sacrificed his eye for wisdom. He sacrificed physical dignity and masculine honor for seidr. He sacrificed his comfort for the runes. He collects dead warriors instead of ruling a paradise. Every choice Odin makes trades something respectable for something useful. Norse mythology's most powerful figure is also its most relentlessly transactional.
facts.visualDescription: A robed one-eyed figure seated on a high ritual platform in women's attire, staff upright before him, head tilted back in trance — and at the base of the platform, a circle of chanting women, their breath visible in cold air, the air between them glowing faintly with threads of fate visible to no one who isn't in the trance
facts.sourceName: Wikipedia — Seiðr (Ynglinga saga; Lokasenna, Poetic Edda)

variants.question: Which goddess first taught seidr magic to the Aesir gods, according to the Ynglinga saga?
What was the Old Norse term for a female seidr practitioner who traveled between settlements?
True or false: Seidr was primarily practiced by women, and Odin's use of it was considered shameful by Norse gender norms.
___ practiced seidr magic despite it being considered unmanly, and was mocked for it by Loki in the Lokasenna.

facts.quizQuestion: Which three beings visited every newborn to determine their fate — power that even gods could not override?
facts.explanation: The Prose Edda's Gylfaginning (chapter 16) describes three maidens from the hall of the Norns who determine the lifespan and fate of every person born into the world. The Norns' authority is absolute — not contingent on approval from Odin, not subject to appeal, not modifiable by human prayer or divine intervention. Even the gods themselves ride over Bifröst daily to hold court at the Well of Urðr, effectively operating under the Norns' supervision. This is why Norse fate is so inescapable and so tragic: it is not set by a god who might be persuaded but by a structural feature of existence that predates and exceeds all divine agency.
facts.statement: The three Norns — Urðr, Verðandi, and Skuld — visit every newborn to determine their fate, and no divine authority can override what the Norns decree.
facts.wowFactor: Every day the gods cross the rainbow bridge to hold council — and the bridge leads to the Norns' well. The most powerful beings in the cosmos begin their governance day at the home of the beings who govern them. Norse mythology places fate structurally above gods in a way Greek or Egyptian mythology does not. Odin can command armies and consult the dead, but he cannot change a single thing the Norns carved.
facts.visualDescription: Three women of different ages gathered around a glowing cradle in a vast dark hall beneath the roots of an ash tree — the eldest's hands already weaving luminous threads around the sleeping child, the middle one measuring and shaping, the youngest with shears ready — their faces calm with the absolute certainty of those who know exactly what they are deciding
facts.sourceName: Wikipedia — Urðarbrunnr (Gylfaginning, Prose Edda; Völuspá, Poetic Edda)

variants.question: The three Norns are named Urðr, Verðandi, and Skuld — where do they live?
The Norn named Skuld is associated with which aspect of time?
True or false: The Norns determine the fate of every person at birth, and even the Norse gods are subject to their decrees.
___ are the three fate-weavers of Norse mythology who determine the lifespan and destiny of every being at birth.

facts.quizQuestion: Which Norse event ends with the world submerged and then reborn, repopulated by two human survivors?
facts.explanation: The Voluspa describes the post-Ragnarök world in detail: the earth rising again from the sea, unsown fields already growing grain, Baldr returning from Helheim alongside Höðr his killer, surviving gods reuniting to find the golden game-pieces from the old world in the grass. Two humans, Líf and Lífþrasir ('Life' and 'Life-Keeper'), emerge from the sacred forest Hoddmímis holt where they sheltered during the apocalypse, eating morning dew for sustenance while the world burned. From them, humanity begins again. The cycle of creation and destruction that began with Odin killing Ymir and building a world from a corpse — covered in the Creation & Ragnarök sub-deck — repeats itself. Níðhöggr survives into the new world, as covered in the myth_norse_nidhogg_ragnarok fact.
facts.statement: Ragnarök is not an ending — it is a reset. The world burns, the gods fall, the earth sinks, and then the world rises again, green and fertile, repopulated by two survivors who sheltered in a sacred forest.
facts.wowFactor: Líf and Lífþrasir survived Ragnarök by eating morning dew. While Odin was swallowed, Thor was poisoned, and Freyr was killed by a fire giant with a flaming sword, two humans crouched in a forest eating condensation off leaves and outlasted the end of the world. Norse mythology's great survivors are not the strongest or the wisest but the ones who happened to be in the right forest.
facts.visualDescription: A vast burning world seen from above — fire at every horizon, ocean flooding the lowlands, the ash of gods and giants rising in columns — and at the center, barely visible, a dark forest still standing, and within it, two small human figures huddled together under ancient branches, alive and waiting for the fire to end
facts.sourceName: Wikipedia — Ragnarök (Poetic Edda; Prose Edda, Snorri Sturluson)
